question: On which region of the displayed image is the primary finding located?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the axial CT image to locate the visually highlighted abnormality. I see a red arrow and the label "1.4 cm" pointing to a focal rounded/tubular structure with adjacent mesenteric fat changes in the lower portion of the image on the viewer's left side.

Next, I relate this observation to the question, which asks which region of the image contains the primary finding. I focus only on the position of the marked finding relative to the image frame (upper vs lower, left vs right).

Then, I compare that position to the provided answer choices. The marked abnormality clearly lies below the horizontal midline of the image and on the left side of the image frame, which corresponds to the "lower left" region.

Therefore, based on the visible arrowed measurement and its location in the image, the primary finding is in the lower left of the image.
caption: Abdominal CT performed with oral and IV contrast. Appendix is dilated to 1.4 cm in diameter with marked mural thickening and mild adjacent fat stranding, typical of acute appendicitis.
sub_caption: Appendix is dilated to 1.4 cm in diameter with marked mural thickening and mild adjacent fat stranding, typical of acute appendicitis.
image_context: The patient was afebrile, nontoxic in appearance, and with stable vital signs. His abdomen was non-distended, tender to deep palpation in the right lower quadrant, without rebound or guarding. Rovsing’s sign was negative. Complete blood count demonstrated a hemoglobin of 14.2, hematocrit of 41.4, white blood cell count of 5.1 × 109/L with neutrophil percentage of 30.1%, and platelet count of 133 × 109/L. Biochemistries and liver function tests were within normal limits. An abdominal/pelvic Computed Tomography (CT) scan with contrast demonstrated a 1.4 cm in diameter appendix with marked mural thickening and mild fat stranding typical of acute appendicitis (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>). The patient underwent a laparoscopic appendectomy. Intraoperative findings were consistent with an inflamed appendix. Postoperative recovery was uncomplicated.). The patient underwent a laparoscopic appendectomy. Intraoperative findings were consistent with an inflamed appendix. Postoperative recovery was uncomplicated.Fig. 1Abdominal CT performed with oral and IV contrast. Appendix is dilated to 1.4 cm in diameter with marked mural thickening and mild adjacent fat stranding, typical of acute appendicitis.Fig. 1
